app.title: Arithmetic — From Adders to Execution
app.subtitle: Everything reduces to addition: how computers do math with just gates and wires
adders.intro: Addition is the foundation of ALL arithmetic in a CPU. Every subtraction, every multiplication, every comparison ultimately flows through an adder circuit. Here we build one from scratch: starting with a half adder (2 gates), scaling to a full adder (5 gates), and chaining them into a ripple-carry adder that adds multi-bit numbers.
adders.half.title: Half Adder — Adding Two Bits
adders.half.description: The simplest arithmetic circuit: adds two single bits. The sum is XOR(A, B) — it's 1 when inputs differ. The carry is AND(A, B) — it's 1 only when both inputs are 1. Called 'half' because it can't accept a carry-in from a previous column.
adders.half.ariaLabel: Half adder: XOR gate for sum, AND gate for carry
adders.full.title: Full Adder — Handling the Carry
adders.full.description: Extends the half adder by accepting a carry-in from the previous bit position. Built from two half adders and an OR gate: first half-add A and B, then half-add that result with carry-in. If either step produces a carry, the final carry-out is 1.
adders.full.ariaLabel: Full adder: two half adders plus OR gate for carry propagation
adders.ripple.title: Ripple-Carry Adder (4-bit)
adders.ripple.description: Chains 4 full adders together, with each carry-out wired to the next carry-in. Just like adding numbers on paper — column by column, right to left, carrying as you go. The carry 'ripples' from the least significant bit all the way to the most significant bit.
adders.ripple.ariaLabel: 4-bit ripple-carry adder: four chained full adders with carry propagation
adders.ripple.overflow: Overflow! Result doesn't fit in 4 bits.
truthTable.title: Truth Table
